us-politics.headline: Trump Escalates Iran Threats as US Jets Shot Down
us-politics.body: President Trump warned Iran that 'time is running out' before 'all hell' rains down, while Senator Lindsey Graham urged Tehran to 'choose wisely' as a diplomatic deadline approaches. Trump has described the conflict as 'near completion,' though the scope of US objectives remains contested. US military jets have been shot down by enemy fire - the first such losses in over 20 years - raising the stakes considerably. Secretary of State Rubio separately revoked the green cards of two relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani living in the US, who were then arrested by ICE. Critics question what 'finishing the job' actually means strategically, while analysts argue the US can prevail through attrition without deploying ground troops.

sources.title: Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’
TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years
The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict
Graham backs Trump’s Iran ultimatum as deadline approaches: ‘Choose wisely’
In Iran, what does ‘finish the job’ even mean?
Trump warns Iran: ‘Time is running out’ before ‘all hell’ rains down

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Proposes Defense Surge, Deep Domestic Cuts
us-politics.body: The White House unveiled a budget blueprint proposing $1.5 trillion in defense spending increases paired with sharp cuts to domestic programs. The proposal reflects Trump's stated priority of military buildup, though Congress will have the final say on appropriations. Separately, a federal judge blocked a Trump administration effort requiring colleges to certify they do not consider race in admissions, handing the administration a legal setback on education policy. Former White House press secretary Kayleigh McEnany warned that the 2026 midterms 'will be hard' for Republicans, a rare public acknowledgment of political vulnerability from within the party's orbit.

sources.title: Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions
McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Trump unveils $1.5T defense surge, deep domestic cuts — what’s on the budget chopping block

us-politics.headline: White House Ballroom Battle Heads Back to Court
us-politics.body: Trump filed an emergency appeal to keep construction of a ballroom inside the White House complex moving forward after a court moved to halt the project. The renovation has drawn scrutiny over its legal basis and use of federal space. Details of the planned structure - described as a bunker-ballroom hybrid - have raised questions about transparency and the scope of executive authority over White House grounds. The legal fight is ongoing.

sources.title: What we know about the White House ballroom bunker
Trump files emergency appeal to keep building White House ballroom

us-politics.headline: Wisconsin Race Exposes GOP Weakness; China Tops US in Global Approval
us-politics.body: A Wisconsin state Supreme Court race is highlighting persistent structural problems for Republicans at the state level, with the party struggling to compete in judicial contests despite national momentum. Separately, a new Gallup poll found China now outranks the United States in global approval ratings - a notable shift that reflects declining international confidence in US leadership. The finding comes as the administration pursues an aggressive foreign policy posture across multiple fronts. Both data points are being watched closely ahead of the 2026 midterm cycle.

sources.title: Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles
China now tops US in global approval ratings: Gallup
McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans

au-politics.headline: Economist warns stagflation here as fuel costs bite
au-politics.body:
A leading economist says Australia is already experiencing stagflation - the toxic combination of stalled growth and rising inflation - as global pressures mount. The warning comes as ordinary Australians report changing their commuting habits due to fuel costs, with some describing food as a 'privilege' they can no longer take for granted.

The economic squeeze is hitting households unevenly. Regional and lower-income Australians appear most exposed, facing higher transport costs with fewer alternatives. Senator David Pocock has separately attacked Labor's gambling reform response as inadequate, arguing the government is tinkering at the edges of a crisis that demands structural change.

sources.title: Stagflation is already happening, a leading economist warns
Fuel prices force commute changes, as food becomes 'privilege' for some
We cannot celebrate tinkering when it comes to gambling reform. Labor’s response falls tragically short | David Pocock

au-politics.headline: NT aviation project moves offshore after millions spent
au-politics.body:
The Northern Territory government's Albatross Aviation project has shifted operations offshore after burning through what critics called 'ridiculous' millions in public money. The project, backed by the NT government, failed to establish a sustainable local base despite significant taxpayer investment.

The move raises fresh questions about government oversight of publicly funded ventures in remote Australia, where infrastructure spending is already under scrutiny. No minister has yet publicly accounted for the decision to relocate operations abroad.

sources.title: After NT spent 'ridiculous' millions on aircraft, operations move offshore

au-politics.headline: Melbourne murder suspect caught fleeing at airport
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport on Saturday while allegedly attempting to board an international flight after murdering a woman. Police intercepted him before he could leave the country.

Details of the victim and the relationship between the two have not yet been released publicly. The arrest prevented what authorities would have treated as a complex international fugitive case.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport after allegedly murdering woman

au-politics.headline: Victoria's First Nations treaty body holds historic elections
au-politics.body:
Victoria's First Nations treaty body has begun its first elections, a milestone in the state's long-running treaty process. The body, made up of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people, will represent First Nations communities in formal negotiations with the Victorian government.

Organisers framed the elections as an act of self-determination. 'We're the experts in our own lives,' one participant said. Victoria remains the only Australian state with a treaty process at this stage of development, though progress has been slow and the path to a final agreement remains long.

sources.title: ‘We’re the experts in our own lives’: Victoria’s First Nations treaty body is holding its first elections

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates With Ultimatum and Missing Pilot
world.body:
The United States and Israel have intensified strikes on Iranian infrastructure, hitting universities, bridges, and a Tehran psychiatric hospital. Trump has given Iran a 48-hour deadline to negotiate a deal, while both sides trade threats of further escalation. A US fighter jet was shot down over Iran, and the search for the missing airman continues, presenting what analysts call a serious diplomatic and military test for Washington.

Off the battlefield, the US is applying pressure through other means. Secretary of State Marco Rubio stripped Qasem Soleimani's niece of her US residency, and American authorities arrested relatives of the late Iranian general. Pakistan, sharing a long border with Iran, faces growing pressure to navigate the fallout from a conflict it did not choose.

sources.title: US and Iran trade threats to unleash 'hell' as search for missing US airman continues
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
Search for missing airman presents serious test for US
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Marco Rubio says he has stripped Qassem Soleimani’s niece of US residency
Trump says Iran has 48 hours to make deal as search for US pilot continues
What the Iran-Iraq war can tell us about the US-Israeli war on Iran

world.headline: Gaza and Lebanon Violence Continues as Protests Erupt in Tel Aviv
world.body:
Israeli drone strikes hit a vehicle in central Gaza and a building in Lebanon's Tyre, adding to a sustained campaign across multiple fronts. Indonesia received the bodies of UN peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon, a stark reminder of the conflict's regional toll. Displaced Lebanese civilians describe lives turned upside down with little prospect of return.

Inside Israel, police arrested antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv, signalling growing domestic dissent. In the occupied West Bank, new Israeli measures are tightening control over Hebron's Ibrahimi Mosque, drawing further condemnation from Palestinian and international observers.

sources.title: Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv
Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Witness records Israeli strike on building in Lebanon’s Tyre
Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Curbs for Young Men
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended stays abroad, a move reflecting Berlin's push to rebuild defence readiness amid the broader European security crisis. The proposal has sparked debate about civil liberties and the pace at which European governments are shifting toward wartime-style mobilisation frameworks.

Separately, Russia killed five people in a strike on a Ukrainian market, continuing its pattern of attacks on civilian infrastructure. The strike drew fresh condemnation but no immediate shift in the military situation on the ground.

sources.title: German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad
Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five

world.headline: Car Ploughs Into Lao New Year Parade, Injuring 15
world.body:
A vehicle drove into a Lao New Year parade in Louisiana, injuring around 15 people. Authorities are investigating the circumstances of the crash, including whether it was deliberate. The incident disrupted celebrations for the local Lao community and prompted a swift emergency response.

In the UK, three suspects were remanded in custody following an attack on a Jewish charity, while the family of the late Virginia Giuffre urged King Charles to meet Epstein survivors during an upcoming US visit, keeping the long-running Epstein scandal in the public eye.

sources.title: Car slams into Louisiana Lao New Year parade, injuring about 15 people
Giuffre family urges UK’s King Charles to meet Epstein survivors in US
Three suspects ordered to stay in UK custody over Jewish charity attack

ai.headline: Anthropic Faces Pricing Backlash, Security Breach, and Market Scrutiny
ai.body:
Anthropic is navigating a rough week. The company announced that Claude Code subscribers will pay extra for OpenClaw support, a move that drew immediate pushback from developers already paying premium prices. Separately, hackers leaked Claude Code source and are distributing it bundled with malware, turning the breach into an active security threat for anyone who downloads unofficial copies.

On the financial side, Anthropic is riding high in private markets, but analysts warn that SpaceX's fundraising appetite could compete for the same investor dollars, potentially cooling the enthusiasm. New Anthropic research published this week also examined how emotion concepts function internally within large language models, adding a rare moment of scientific credibility to an otherwise turbulent news cycle.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model

ai.headline: AI Fakes, Copyright Trolls, and the Fight to Prove Humanity
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell found herself targeted by both AI-generated fakes of her music and a copyright troll exploiting the confusion, illustrating how the two threats now reinforce each other. The case highlights a growing legal vacuum where artists have little recourse against synthetic imitation.

The authenticity problem is spreading beyond music. Creators across disciplines are now being asked to prove their work is human-made, prompting a nascent market for "human-made" certification logos and labels. Meanwhile, Meta enforced a legal gag order against Sarah Wynn-Williams, author of "Careless People," barring her from making negative public statements about the company - a reminder that corporate suppression of critical voices is keeping pace with AI-driven disinformation.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it
Author of "Careless People" banned from saying anything negative about Meta

ai.headline: GPU Access Expands as AI Infrastructure Pressure Mounts
ai.body:
Apple approved a driver enabling Nvidia external GPUs to work with Arm Macs, opening a meaningful upgrade path for developers locked out of high-end local compute. The move is modest but signals softening in Apple's historically closed hardware ecosystem.

On the software side, a new tool called sllm lets developers split a GPU node and share access with others, pitching unlimited tokens as the draw. A separate browser-based project brought Google's TurboQuant vector quantization to WebAssembly, pushing inference closer to the edge. Taken together, the week showed sustained grassroots pressure to democratize GPU access as centralized cloud costs remain high.

sources.title: Apple approves driver that lets Nvidia eGPUs work with Arm Macs
Show HN: sllm – Split a GPU node with other developers, unlimited tokens
Show HN: TurboQuant-WASM – Google's vector quantization in the browser

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair and Digital ID Laws Face Industry Pushback
ai.body:
Tech companies are lobbying hard to gut Colorado's right-to-repair law, one of the strongest in the United States, by pushing amendments that would carve out broad exemptions and limit consumer remedies. Critics say the amendments would render the law largely symbolic.

In Europe, Germany's implementation of the eIDAS digital identity standard is drawing scrutiny for a different reason: the architecture requires users to have an Apple or Google account to function, effectively handing two American platform giants control over a core piece of European digital infrastructure. Both stories reflect the same pattern - legislation designed to empower individuals being quietly reshaped to serve incumbent platforms.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

btc.headline: Bitcoin Gains Political and Institutional Credibility After Global Shocks
btc.body:
A former UK Chancellor has publicly backed Bitcoin as an alternative to what he calls failing financial systems, adding political weight to a growing institutional narrative. Research from Mercado Bitcoin reinforces the case: Bitcoin has historically outperformed both gold and equities in the aftermath of major global shocks, recovering faster and delivering stronger returns.

The timing matters. With macro uncertainty elevated, the argument for Bitcoin as a crisis-resistant asset is moving from crypto-native circles into mainstream finance and politics. That shift in who is making the argument - and where - signals something beyond a typical bull-market talking point.

sources.title: Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds
Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems

btc.headline: Digital Asset Treasuries Face Pressure to Justify Their Existence
btc.body:
Corporate Bitcoin and crypto treasuries are under scrutiny. A new opinion piece argues these holdings can no longer coast on price appreciation alone - they need to generate yield, demonstrate strategy, or face questions about capital allocation discipline.

The critique lands at a moment when 24/7 stock trading is also gaining traction, with traders welcoming the end of after-hours price gaps that have long favored institutional players. Together, these developments point to a market structure growing more demanding and more continuous. Assets and the companies holding them will need to perform in real time.

sources.title: Digital asset treasuries must now earn their keep
Traders are the big winners as 24/7 stocks will finally end the after-hours price 'manipulation'

btc.headline: Jimmy Song Pushes for a More Conservative Bitcoin Node Client
btc.body:
Developer and Bitcoin educator Jimmy Song is calling for a dedicated node client built around conservative principles - one that resists protocol changes more aggressively than existing implementations. His argument centers on the idea that Bitcoin's value depends on predictability, and that current clients leave too much room for contentious upgrades to gain traction.

The proposal is unlikely to win universal support, but it reflects a real tension inside Bitcoin development between those who want the protocol to evolve and those who see stability as the product itself.

sources.title: Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client

btc.headline: Nevada Court Blocks Kalshi as Prediction Market Legal Battles Intensify
btc.body:
A Nevada judge has extended a ban on Kalshi operating in the state, rejecting the platform's argument that its event contracts fall outside gambling regulations. The ruling is a setback for prediction markets broadly, which have spent the past year pushing regulators to treat them as financial instruments rather than gambling products.

Kalshi has federal CFTC authorization, but state-level resistance is proving stubborn. The Nevada decision signals that federal approval alone may not be enough to unlock the US market.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory tart with caramelized leeks and umami-rich mushrooms folded in buttery rough-puff pastry
food.tip: Brush the miso glaze on the filling twice — once before baking and once in the final five minutes — to build a lacquered, deeply savory crust without burning

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that climbs through four effort levels — easy jog, conversational pace, comfortably hard, and near-threshold — spending eight minutes at each before descending back down, training your body to shift gears rather than just grind at one speed

learn.topic: Eigenvalues and the hidden geometry of Google's PageRank
learn.description: PageRank — the algorithm that made Google — is essentially a problem of finding the dominant eigenvector of a massive matrix representing the web's link structure, meaning the 'importance' of every page is a geometric fixed point the algorithm converges toward
learn.why: Understanding this collapses the apparent magic of search ranking into a single elegant idea from linear algebra, and once you see eigenvectors this way you start noticing the same concept quietly running facial recognition, quantum mechanics, and structural engineering
